1:
The chapter opens on holy ground, and the witnesses gather first around the place — Mount Moriah — and the freight of memory it carries. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch survey the whole design before entering it: the account begins with the place and the time of building, then the proportions, and so advances from the outside inward — first the porch, then the house or holy place, then the holiest of all with its cherubim, and the veil between; after which come the furnishings of the court, the pillars of the porch, the brazen altar, the brazen sea, the ten lavers, the candlesticks and tables, and finally the enumeration of the temple's vessels. It is an ordered progress from threshold to inmost shrine.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry dwells on why this ground and no other. Solomon was neither free to choose nor at a loss to fix the site, for it had been determined beforehand. It must be at Jerusalem, where God had chosen to put his name, so that the royal city should be the holy city and the seat of the thrones of judgment, as Ps. 122:4-5 sings. It must be on Moriah, which some take to be the very place in the land of Moriah where Abraham was bound to offer Isaac in Gen. 22:2 — the ram provided in his stead a type of Christ's own sacrifice, so that the temple, itself a type of Christ, was fitly built there. It must be where the LORD had appeared to David and answered him by fire at 1 Chron. 21:26, where atonement had once been made and must still be made; and in the place David had prepared, not merely purchased but pitched upon by divine direction, so that Solomon's wisdom lay in acquiescing rather than seeking a more convenient spot. Even the threshing-floor of Ornan, a Jebusite's, speaks — an encouragement to the Gentiles, and a reminder that temple-work is threshing-work, the labour of the mind.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke confirms the tradition from the Targum, which makes this the place where Abraham prayed and worshipped, where all generations shall worship, where Isaac was bound and the ram substituted, where Jacob prayed in his flight from Esau, and where the angel of the LORD appeared to David when he built his altar in the threshing-floor bought from Araunah. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds that nowhere else in the Old Testament is the temple site so precisely named, and hears in Moriah an allusion to its own etymology, the "appearance of Jah" — reading the words "who appeared unto David his father" and translating "in the mount of the Appearance of Jah," a sense he prefers to the Vulgate's "which had been shown." The reformed John Gill, for his part, refers the reader to his chapter introduction. Together the voices make the opening verse a convergence of every earlier meeting between God and man upon that hill.
2:
With the site settled, the record turns to the date, and the witnesses divide their attention between the calendar and its small textual wrinkle. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the notice plainly: Solomon began to build in the second month of the fourth year of his reign, and they observe that the historian gives only the length and breadth of the house, passing over the height that the parallel supplies at 1 Kings 6:2. Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, presses the wording. The Hebrew reads awkwardly, "in the second month in the second," a repetition the versions omit and which he judges a scribe's error; "on the second day" would have been phrased differently. He would read simply, "he began to build in the second month" — that is, in Zif, the old name for April to May, exactly as 1 Kings 6:1 has it.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the bare date into reflection. The work began not until the fourth year, yet the first three years were not trifled away nor spent debating whether to build at all; they went in the necessary preparations, in which three years would soon pass, considering how many hands had to be gathered and set to work. Henry offers a further conjecture: that this may have been a sabbatical year, a year of release when the people, discharged from their husbandry, could more easily lend a hand to the beginning of the work — and that the year of its finishing would likewise fall out to be a sabbatical year, when they would again have leisure to attend the solemnity of its dedication.

The voices do not contend. The two grammatical-historical witnesses attend to the letter — the month, the suspected copyist's slip, the height left unrecorded — while the puritan reads the providence in the timing, the patient years of preparation that no idle delay but honest labour filled. Between them the verse yields both its precise chronology and its quiet lesson, that the house of God was begun in due season and with due deliberation, every hand made ready before the first stone was laid. What Henry calls preparation the grammarians call foundation, and the next verse will take up the measure of that foundation in cubits.
3:
Sixty cubits long and twenty broad — the dimensions of the foundation come with a curious editorial aside, "after the first measure," and the witnesses fasten on that phrase as the key to the whole verse. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the caution behind it. The "first measure" means the cubit used in the days of Moses, set in deliberate contrast to the cubit the Israelites adopted in Babylon and carried home after the captivity; and because the books of Chronicles were written after that captivity, the writer had to specify which standard he meant, lest his readers reckon by the Babylonish cubit, which was a palm — a full sixth — shorter than the cubit of Moses. Clarke points to the same distinction drawn by Ezekiel at Ezek. 40:5 and Ezek. 43:13.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms both points from the Hebrew. The opening clause, rendered in the older versions "these are the things wherein Solomon was instructed," he would translate rather "this is the foundation," or ground-plan, of Solomon — the plural pronoun standing for a neuter singular, as at 1 Chronicles 24:19, and the infinitive "to be founded" used as a noun, as in Ezra 3:11, which the Vulgate supports. "After the first measure" he reads "in the ancient measure," an explanation absent from the parallel at 1 Kings 6:2; the ancient or Mosaic cubit was one handbreadth longer than the cubit of later times, again on the warrant of Ezekiel. And he notes what the chronicler has quietly dropped: the height of thirty cubits that the parallel account preserves.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds only a brief gloss that widens the frame. "Instructed," he says, means instructed by David, and by the Spirit of God; and "the measure" is the measure that was first fixed. The witnesses do not divide. Together they make this verse a small monument to the chronicler's care — a writer conscious of standing on the far side of the exile, translating an old builder's cubits for a later generation, and grounding even the arithmetic of the house in a plan first given by God through David.
4:
The porch draws the witnesses into their first real dispute, for the text gives its height as "an hundred and twenty," and that towering figure sits uneasily beside everything around it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe the structure without alarm: the porch stood in front of the house and matched its breadth, twenty cubits across, and was overlaid within with pure gold; the phrase "in front" governs both its position before the house and its measurement across the front. But the height is another matter. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays the problem out squarely. Some think the number should be twenty only; and if this is the same building described at 1 Kings 6:2, the house itself was but thirty cubits high, so that a porch of a hundred and twenty would overtop it fourfold. Twenty, he notes, is the reading of the Syriac, the Arabic, and the Septuagint in the Codex Alexandrinus, though the Hebrew manuscripts give no help.

Clarke proposes a mechanism for the slip. The Hebrew words as they stand read "a hundred and twenty"; but the letters of meah, a hundred, are easily transposed with those of ammah, a cubit — set the aleph after the mem and the word is "hundred," set it before and the word is "cubit" — so that the original may have read "twenty cubits," exactly as the Syriac, Arabic, and Septuagint took it. This, he urges, would bring the porch within the proportion of the other measures, whereas a hundred and twenty seems far too great. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reasons to the same end from a different angle: a hundred and twenty would make the porch four times the height of the main building; the Alexandrine Septuagint and the Arabic read "twenty cubits"; the Syriac omits the whole clause, which has no parallel in the Kings account and is further suspect for lacking the usual word "cubits" after the number. He even wonders whether the Hebrew is a corruption of "and its breadth ten cubits," and confirms that the porch was overlaid within with pure gold, as at 1 Kings 6:21.

Against this near-consensus that the figure is an error stands the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, who takes the height at face value and accounts for it: the porch was "a kind of turret to the building," a tower rising well above the house behind it. So the debate is genuine — whether "a hundred and twenty" is a copyist's stumble over two similar Hebrew words, to be corrected to twenty on the witness of the versions, or a true measurement of a porch built up like a tower before the temple. The witnesses leave both readings on the table, the grammarians pressing for emendation and Wesley content to let the great number stand.
5:
From the porch the description moves inward to the main hall, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses labour over its panelling and its ornament. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the "great house" as the large apartment of the building, the holy place, which Solomon wainscotted with cypress, overlaid with good gold, and carved with palms and garlands. The "palms" they take as artificial palm-trees worked into the walls as ornaments, comparing Ezekiel 41:18; the "chains" are spiral, chain-like decorations, garlands of carved flowers such as the parallel describes at 1 Kings 6:18.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, works the same details with a lexicographer's patience. The "greater house" is the great chamber, the Holy Place or nave, named also at 1 Chronicles 28:11; "cieled with fir" means covered with planks of fir, or panelled — for to ciel, or rather seel, a room meant in old English to wainscot it, as at 1 Kings 6:15-16. The cypress panelling was then plated with fine gold. The "palm trees and chains" he renders as palms and chain-work carved, or brought up, upon the wood, citing 1 Kings 7:17 for the chains and 1 Kings 6:29 for the palms; the chain-work must have been garland-like carvings on the fir panels. He notes that 1 Kings 6:18 omits mention of the chains altogether, while the Septuagint reads "carved on it palms and chains," the Syriac "figured on it the likeness of palms and lilies," and the Vulgate "graved on it palms and as it were chainlets intertwining."

The witnesses do not divide; they simply build up, plank by gilded plank, a single picture of the inner hall. A room of cypress overlaid with gold, its walls alive with carved palms and looping garlands — the two grammarians agree on every particular, differing only in the range of ancient versions each brings to bear. What emerges is the deliberate richness of the holy place: no bare stone, but a chamber wrought and plated so that the very timber disappeared beneath gold, and the walls seemed a garden of palms, a fit approach to the shrine that lay beyond.
6:
A single exotic word — Parvaim — sets the witnesses hunting across the map, for the verse says the gold that garnished the house came from a place that appears nowhere else in Scripture. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch begin with the garnishing itself: the house was ornamented with precious stones set into the inner walls, a practice they find entirely credible, since among the treasures Solomon brought in quantity from Ophir precious stones are expressly named at 1 Kings 10:11, and it was a common custom in the East to adorn buildings and vessels so; they recall the store David had gathered at 1 Chronicles 29:2. As for the gold of Parvaim, they confess the name has never been satisfactorily explained: Gesenius compares the Sanskrit parvam, "foremost," and takes it for the eastern regions, while others hold it to be some city in southern or eastern Arabia through which Indian gold reached Palestine.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke widens the guesswork. He too admits he does not know the place; some identify it with Sepharvaim in Armenia or Media, conquered by the king of Assyria at 2 Kings 17:24; others with Taprobane, the island of Ceylon, deriving the name from taph, a border, and Parvan, the coast of Parvan. The rabbins, he adds, thought it gold of a blood-red colour, named from parim, bullocks, as being like bullocks' blood — while the Vulgate abandons Parvaim altogether and renders the clause of a pavement of most precious marble, with gold of the best quality.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gathers the threads. "Garnished" he reads as "overlaid" the chamber; the precious stones he ties again to 1 Chronicles 29:2 and to the cargo of precious stones Hiram's fleet brought from Ophir, noting that the Kings account makes no mention of this kind of decoration, and that the Vulgate here too imagines a floor of costly marble. Parvaim itself he guesses may be Farwa, an auriferous region of southern Arabia, though others connect the word with the Sanskrit purva, "eastern," and seek it, like Ophir, in India; in any case, he grants, the name does not recur in the Old Testament. The witnesses do not divide over anything of substance; they simply confess, each in his own learning, that the source of the temple's finest gold has slipped out of memory, leaving only a beautiful and untraceable name.
7:
The gilding now spreads to every surface, and with it the first of the temple's carved cherubim, but the verse also carries the witnesses into a small quarrel with a modern critic. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the plain sense: Solomon overlaid the house — its beams, its posts, its walls, and its doors — with gold, and graved cherubim upon the walls. Their eye is already moving to the most holy place beyond, where the account will trade the unspoken height for the weight of the gold; but here they mark simply the total covering of the structure and the figures cut into its walls.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, sharpens each term. "He overlaid the house" means he covered the great chamber, the Holy Place, as at 1 Kings 6:21-23; the "beams" are those of the roof, the "posts" rather the thresholds, the word he finds at Isaiah 6:4; and the graved cherubim answer to the fuller account of the mural carvings at 1 Kings 6:29. On the word "cherubim" he lingers, noting that it is the Hebrew plural, beside which stands the Aramaic form "cherubin" preserved in the Te Deum, and he even reaches for Shakespeare's line in Cymbeline of a roof "with golden cherubins fretted." Then he turns to defend the chronicler: he cannot see why the critic Reuss should call this sketch of the porch and nave "confused," finding the charge unwarranted.

The two witnesses do not divide, and their agreement carries a quiet apologetic. Where a modern reader like Reuss sees only a muddled abridgement of the older narrative, the grammarians find an orderly and intelligible account — gold upon beam and post and wall and door, and the guardian cherubim graven over all, exactly as a careful writer working from fuller records might set it down. The verse thus does double duty: it completes the picture of a house wholly sheathed in gold and adorned with the figures of heaven's attendants, and it lets the witnesses insist, against the charge of confusion, that the chronicler knew precisely what he was describing.
8:
The account now crosses the threshold into the innermost room, and the witnesses measure both its perfect shape and the staggering weight of gold that clothed it. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott names it the most holy house — the chamber of the Holy of Holies, the chancel or oracle, the Debir of 1 Kings 6:5, a term he will meet again at v.10. Its length, set against the breadth of the house, was twenty cubits, measured just as the porch had been; and its breadth was likewise twenty cubits. Ellicott draws in the parallel to complete the figure: 1 Kings 6:20 adds that the height too was twenty cubits, so that the chamber formed a perfect cube — a room as tall as it was long and broad, the geometry of completeness at the heart of the temple.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, read the room the same way, noting that its length was reckoned in front of the breadth of the house, twenty cubits, exactly as the porch at v.4, and its depth twenty cubits also. They observe what the chronicler substitutes for the height that the Kings account preserves: in place of the missing measurement he records the weight of the gold, six hundred talents laid upon the walls of the shrine — a figure the older narrative omits.

It is that figure the witnesses will not let pass without a gasp. Ellicott sets it beside a telling comparison: six hundred talents of gold spent on the plating of a single room, when Solomon's whole yearly revenue, by 1 Kings 10:14, came to six hundred and sixty-six talents. The most holy place, then, swallowed in its gilding very nearly a full year's income of the wealthiest king in Israel's memory. The cube itself the grammarians record without allegory, content to note that the room where God would dwell was built to a measure of perfect equality on every side, twenty cubits by twenty by twenty; and against that equal room the chronicler weighs, not a height, but a treasure. The witnesses do not divide; they let the cube of the oracle and the mountain of gold speak together of a room built without measure and without stint, the costliest and most exactly proportioned space in the whole house, made ready for the presence that would fill it.
9:
Even the fastenings of the temple are weighed in gold, and the witnesses divide gently over what these "nails" were and how the fifty shekels are to be reckoned. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke objects to the very word: bolts, he insists, must be meant, for it would be preposterous to suppose nails of nearly two pounds' weight apiece; and the "upper chambers" gilded alongside them he takes to be the ceiling. His fellow wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the number as value rather than mere mass — each nail, screw, or pin by which the golden plates were fastened to the walls was worth fifty shekels, workmanship and all — and he too glosses the upper chambers as the roof.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott settles the grammar and defends the sense. The Hebrew reads, literally, "a weight for nails for shekels — fifty in gold"; the Septuagint and Vulgate understand that the weight of each nail was fifty shekels, and this, he judges, is probably right, since fifty shekels taken as a grand total would be a trifling sum to record beside the six hundred talents just named. The nails, he explains, served to fasten the golden plates to the wooden wainscoting. And whatever one makes of the seemingly incredible quantities of gold and silver David is said to have amassed at 1 Chronicles 22:14 and 1 Chronicles 29:4, no small amount would be needed for such plating; the Babylonian and Assyrian kings, he notes from their own monuments, boasted of clothing the houses of their gods in silver and gold just so. The gilded upper chambers, mentioned at 1 Chronicles 28:11, and indeed both statements of this verse, are peculiar to the chronicler, and the Syriac and Arabic omit the verse entirely.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply confirm the reckoning — fifty shekels of gold for the nails, and the upper chambers overlaid with gold — before passing on to the cherubim. The witnesses do not finally divide: whether one calls them nails or bolts, whether the fifty shekels weigh each pin or all together, they agree on the point that matters, that a house whose very fastenings were counted in gold was built with a lavishness answering to the God who was to dwell in it. Even the smallest hardware of the sanctuary was made precious.
10:
Two colossal cherubim now rise within the most holy place, and the witnesses read them at once as a matter of carpentry, of textual nicety, and of worship. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets them in their history. There were already two cherubim over the ark, small ones whose wings covered the mercy-seat; but now that the most holy place was enlarged, though those old ones were kept as appurtenances of the ark that was not to be made new, two great new cherubim were added, by divine appointment, to fill a room that would otherwise have looked bare. They were of image-work, Henry thinks, designed to represent the angels that attend the divine Majesty; each wing stretched five cubits, so the whole span was twenty, just the breadth of the room at v.8, and they stood upon their feet as servants, their faces turned inward toward the ark at v.13.

Henry draws the devotion out of the posture. They were not set there to be adored, for then they would have been made sitting, as on a throne, with faces toward their worshippers; rather they stood as attendants upon the invisible God. "We must not worship angels," he warns, "but we must worship with angels," for we are come into communion with them at Hebrews 12:22 and must do God's will as they do it; the thought that we worship before him whom the angels adore should fill every approach with reverence, and he bids the reader compare 1 Corinthians 11:10 with Isaiah 6:2.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott turns to the material and the odd word for it. The cherubim, he notes from 1 Kings 6:23-28, were made of oleaster and plated with gold; "of image work" is literally "a work of statuary," a Hebrew term that occurs here only and looks suspicious to him — the Vulgate renders it "statuary work," the Septuagint "a work of logs," the Syriac "a durable work" — and the Greek rendering leads him to suspect the original may have read simply "woodwork," answering to the blocks of oleaster in the Kings account. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads "image work" differently again, as "moveable work": these cherubim, unlike the Mosaic pair fixed to the mercy-seat, stood free in a moving posture, and like Henry he takes them to represent the angels who attend the Divine Majesty. The witnesses converge more than they part — great winged figures, carved of hard wood and sheathed in gold, standing as heaven's servants about the throne of the unseen God, and teaching the worshipper below to lift his heart among the angels.
11:
The verse begins to spell out the great wingspan in careful arithmetic, and the two grammatical-historical witnesses trace the geometry wing by wing. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the total length of the wings as twenty cubits: one wing of the first cherub, five cubits, reached to the wall of the house, and its other wing, also five cubits, reached to the wing of the second cherub. They note that the repeated clauses have a distributive force — what is said of the one cherub is said again of the other, so that the two figures together, each with a wing to the wall and a wing to its fellow, exactly span the room.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, works the same sum from the whole to the parts. The wings were altogether twenty cubits long, so that, outspread, they reached from wall to wall of the Holy of Holies, which was twenty cubits wide. Of that breadth each cherub covered half, or ten cubits, with his two wings of five cubits apiece; and plainly, he observes, the inner wing of each cherub met the inner wing of the other in the very middle of the wall, where the two touched tip to tip.

The witnesses do not divide at all; they simply confirm one another's measurements. What both are describing is a single unbroken rank of wing stretched clean across the inner shrine — the outer wing of each great figure brushing a side wall, the inner wings meeting at the centre, so that the whole twenty-cubit breadth of the most holy place was overshadowed by an unbroken canopy of gold-plated wing. The arithmetic falls out the same in both hands because the fact is plain and fixed: two figures, four wings of five cubits each, twenty cubits in all, and a room exactly twenty cubits wide. The precision is not idle. It shows the two new cherubim so exactly proportioned to the room that they filled it entirely, guardian wings spanning the space above the ark from one wall to the other, leaving no part of the holy place unshadowed by the attendant figures of heaven; the measure of the wing is the measure of the shrine, and the one was clearly wrought to fit the other.
12:
The measuring continues with the second cherub, and the witnesses find in the doubled wording not tedium but a deliberate symmetry. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the verse almost word for word from the Hebrew: the wing of the one cherub, five cubits, was touching the wall of the chamber, and its other wing, likewise five cubits, was cleaving to the wing of the other cherub. The verb he chooses — "cleaving" — catches the closeness of the join, wing pressed hard against wing at the centre of the room; where an earlier clause had the wings simply reaching or touching, here they adhere, so that the two spans are made one continuous canopy. Nothing is added to the picture of the previous verse; it is completed, the mirror-half of a single ordered design.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, had already explained why the clauses run in this repeated way. The repetition carries a distributive force, describing each of the two cherubim in turn — the one with a wing to the wall and a wing to its fellow, and the other in the answering posture — so that the reader sees both figures and understands that the pair, set side by side, reach unbroken across the shrine. The very monotony of "five cubits... five cubits" is the measure of an exact fit, the same span told twice because the two great figures are twins.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the arithmetic of these verses into one image: each wing extending five cubits, the whole made twenty, which was just the breadth of the most holy place at v.8. He will not leave the numbers as numbers. The two great figures, wing meeting wing above the ark, stood as servants about the seat of God, and their outstretched span, filling the room from side to side, taught the worshipper that he drew near a presence attended by the whole host of heaven. The witnesses do not divide; the careful, repeated cubits of this verse are, in their reading, the very proof of a design in which nothing was left bare and nothing left to chance — the guardian wings fitted to the shrine as exactly as the shrine itself was fitted for the God who would meet his people there.
13:
The great figures are now seen whole — wings outspread, standing upright, their faces set a particular way — and the witnesses attend to each of these details. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening "these wings of the cherubim" and reads "spread themselves forth" as a participle, "were outspreading," comparing 1 Chronicles 28:18; "they stood" he takes as "were standing," the figures being ten cubits high, as the parallel notes at 1 Kings 6:26. The crucial word is "inward," which he would translate "toward the chamber": these cherubim did not face one another, as the small cherubim on the mercy-seat did at Exodus 25:20, but looked outward into the room.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, confirm the picture in brief: the wings spread themselves forth the full twenty cubits, and the cherubim stood on their feet with their faces turned inward. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, who had reckoned the same span, draws the theology from the posture. That they stood upon their feet marked them as servants, not enthroned deities; that their faces were turned inward, toward the ark and the presence it signified, showed they were not set there to be adored — for then they would have been made sitting, faces toward their worshippers — but placed as attendants upon the invisible God.

Here the witnesses' small difference over the direction of the faces opens onto a shared meaning. Whether one reads the faces as turned "toward the chamber" with Ellicott or "inward" toward the ark with Henry and the others, the point is the same: these are ministering figures, poised and standing, their whole bearing bent upon the holy place they guard. Unlike the older cherubim that bowed face to face over the mercy-seat, these tower upright across the room, ten cubits high, a standing guard of gold. The very posture teaches, in Henry's reading, what no inscription could: that the room is not a shrine for the images themselves but a throne-room attended by servants who look toward the seat of the unseen King. The witnesses do not divide; together they leave the reader with two colossal servants, wings filling the shrine and faces fixed upon the seat of God, teaching by their very stance that all who enter that room come before a throne attended, not empty.
14:
Between the holy place and the most holy hangs the veil, and the witnesses read in it both the craftsmanship of the tabernacle renewed and the whole distance of the old covenant. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies it as the Parokheth, the curtain that divided the holy place from the Holy of Holies — a veil not named in the present text of 1 Kings 6:21, though that passage speaks of the golden chains by which it was probably suspended. It was of blue, and purple, and crimson, and fine linen, the same materials he had discussed at 2 Chronicles 2:7; and "wrought" with cherubim means raised figures in tapestry or broidered work, exactly as Exodus 26:31 describes the veil of the tabernacle. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, make the same identification: a veil of the very colours and materials of the Mosaic curtain, inwoven with the same cherub figures.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry press past the fabric to its meaning. This inner veil before the most holy place, they say, denoted the darkness of that dispensation and the distance at which the worshippers were kept — shut out from the presence, permitted only to the outer room. But both fix their gaze on what happened to that curtain long after: at the death of Christ the veil was rent, and through him the worshipper is brought nigh, given boldness and liberty not only to look but to enter into the holiest. Henry adds a further touch from the cherubim wrought upon it, reading the Hebrew "he caused them to ascend" as raised, embossed work — figures in an ascending posture, as if to lift the worshipper's heart upward in his devotions.

The witnesses do not divide. The grammarians establish what the veil was — a faithful copy of the tabernacle's parting curtain, cherub-woven, blue and purple and crimson — and the puritan and the Wesleyan tell what it meant, and what became of it. Together they turn a hanging of cloth into a sermon: here the way into God's presence was barred and curtained, guarded even in its embroidery by the same angelic figures that stood in gold within; and here, in the fullness of time, that barrier would be torn from top to bottom, so that the room no eye might enter should be opened by the death of Christ.
15:
The account steps back out of the house to the forecourt, where two great brazen pillars stood, and at once the witnesses meet another troublesome number: their height is given as thirty-five cubits. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch place the pillars before the house, that is, before the porch, referring for their fuller form to 1 Kings 7:15-22. But the thirty-five they cannot accept: elsewhere — at 1 Kings 7:15, at 2 Kings 25:17, and at Jeremiah 52:21 — the pillars measure eighteen cubits, and they explain the higher figure as arising from confounding the Hebrew letters for eighteen with those for thirty-five, a confusion they discuss at 1 Kings 7:16. The capital, or hood, that crowned each pillar they reckon at five cubits. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, agrees flatly: the pillars were thirty and five cubits in length here, but 1 Kings 7:15, 2 Kings 25:17, and Jeremiah 52:21 say eighteen, "no doubt correctly" — and he notes that the Septuagint and Vulgate carry the thirty-five while the Syriac and Arabic keep the eighteen. The "chapiter" is simply the capital, literally the ornament, the "crown" of 1 Kings 7:16.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, however, saves the thirty-five by a different reckoning. He takes the two pillars together to be "somewhat above thirty-five cubits in length," and each of them about eighteen cubits high apiece — so that the larger number is not a single pillar's height at all, but the combined measure of the pair, agreeing at once with the eighteen of the parallel passages.

So the debate is real, though it turns on how to keep the eighteen. Both the grammarians and the puritan honour the eighteen-cubit height fixed in the parallels; but where Keil and Ellicott judge "thirty-five" a copyist's error, born of two similar Hebrew ciphers, Henry reads it as a true figure for the two shafts reckoned together. The witnesses leave the number contested — a scribe's slip to be corrected, or a total to be rightly divided — while agreeing that each pillar rose some eighteen cubits, crowned with a capital of five, before the porch of the house.
16:
The pillars are dressed with chain-work and pomegranates, and here the witnesses meet a phrase so corrupt that they part company over how to mend it. The verse reads, in the older rendering, that Solomon "made chains, as in the oracle," but the Hebrew literally says "chainwork in the oracle" — that is, in the most holy place — and both witnesses agree the words as they stand make no sense, since what is being described is not the inner shrine but an ornament on the capitals of the pillars. There the agreement ends. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch cut the knot by reading the troublesome word as a collar, a Halsreife: Solomon made little chains upon the collar of each capital and set them on the tops of the pillars, with a hundred pomegranates upon the chains. They reconstruct the capital from 1 Kings 7:17-20 and 2 Chronicles 4:12-13 as having two parts, the lower a rounded band a cubit high, covered with chain-like net-work and set with a row of carved pomegranates above and below.

Charles Ellicott, of the same grammatical-historical school, resists that surgery. Rather than change the word into "collar," as he says most commentators have done, he would alter only a single Hebrew letter, reading kad-debir for bad-debir — "chainwork as in the oracle" — an infinitesimal change that recovers the sense the older version already suggests. The chronicler has not told us in so many words that the walls of the Holy of Holies were so ornamented, Ellicott grants, but he stated as much of the great hall at v.5, and 1 Kings 6:29 declares the whole house adorned with mural carvings, so it was natural to write "chainwork such as in the oracle." He notes that the Septuagint simply transliterated the puzzling word and that the Syriac and Arabic guessed at "chains of fifty cubits."

On the pomegranates the witnesses are one. The hundred of this verse, Ellicott shows from Jeremiah 52:23 and 1 Kings 7:20, was but one of four rows, an upper and a lower hundred on the chainwork of each pillar, four hundred in all, as 2 Chronicles 4:13 confirms. So the debate is narrow but genuine: faced with a plainly damaged text, Keil emends the word to "collar" and hangs the chains on the neck of the capital, while Ellicott, changing but a letter, reads "as in the oracle" and defends the received consonants against the very correction his colleagues prefer. Both agree the chains and their pomegranates crowned the pillars; they differ only on which small repair the corrupted Hebrew requires.
17:
The chapter closes with the raising and naming of the two pillars, and the witnesses gather to interpret the names and to answer, once more, a modern detractor. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the bare act: Solomon reared up the pillars before the temple, one on the right hand and one on the left, calling the right-hand pillar Jachin and the left-hand Boaz, and for their position and their names they refer the reader to 1 Kings 7:21. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the meaning from the Targum: the name of the right-hand pillar was Jachin, "because the kingdom of the house of David was established," and the name of the left was Boaz, taken from Boaz the patriarch of the family of Judah, from whom all the kings of the house of Judah descended. So the two names, "he shall establish" and "in it is strength," were read as a standing testimony to the founding and the strength of the royal house that had built the temple.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the setting and adds a note the Kings account preserves: the pillars stood before the temple, in the porch or vestibule, as 1 Kings 7:21 has it, and 1 Kings 7:22 adds that upon their tops was lily-work. Then he turns to defend the chronicler. The critic Reuss had said that the description of the two brazen pillars, told at much greater length in the older narrative, "has become almost unintelligible under the pen of the abbreviator" — a judgment Ellicott calls a strong exaggeration. The same critic had pronounced the disputed word of v.16 "absolutely unintelligible" and foreign to the text; and Ellicott, having already shown how small a change restores its sense, lets his earlier note stand as his reply.

The witnesses do not divide. Keil gives the fact, Clarke the significance drawn from ancient tradition, and Ellicott the setting and the defence; and together they leave the two pillars standing at the temple's threshold as more than ornament. Jachin and Boaz, established and strong, crowned with lily-work and named for the founding and firmness of David's line, frame the entrance of the house — a fitting close to a chapter that began on the hill where God met David, and ends at the door his son built, inscribed in bronze with the promise that the kingdom and its sanctuary alike would stand.
